examples.title: Basic: Converting Requirements to Class Design
examples.description: Demonstrates how to transform simple user requirements into a basic class design model
examples.code:
# Requirement: A library management system needs to manage books
# Step 1: Identify nouns from requirements -> Candidate classes
# - Book, Library, Member
# Step 2: Identify verbs -> Candidate methods
# - add_book(), borrow_book(), return_book()

class Book:
    def __init__(self, book_id, title, author, isbn):
        self.book_id = book_id
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.isbn = isbn
        self.is_available = True
    
    def display_info(self):
        return f"Book: {self.title} by {self.author}"

class Library:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.books = []
        self.members = []
    
    def add_book(self, book):
        self.books.append(book)
        print(f"Added: {book.title}")
    
    def borrow_book(self, book_id, member):
        for book in self.books:
            if book.book_id == book_id and book.is_available:
                book.is_available = False
                print(f"{member.name} borrowed {book.title}")
                return True
        return False

class Member:
    def __init__(self, member_id, name):
        self.member_id = member_id
        self.name = name
        self.borrowed_books = []

# Testing the design model
library = Library("Central Library")
b1 = Book("B001", "Python Basics", "John Smith", "978-0-13-468599-1")
m1 = Member("M001", "Alice")
library.add_book(b1)
library.borrow_book("B001", m1)
examples.explanation: This example shows the basic requirement-to-design transformation: 1) Extract nouns from requirements to identify classes (Book, Library, Member), 2) Extract verbs to identify methods (add_book, borrow_book), 3) Define attributes for each class, 4) Implement the classes with proper encapsulation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of books/members

examples.title: Intermediate: Requirements Analysis to UML-style Design
examples.description: Shows how to analyze complex requirements and create a comprehensive design model with relationships
examples.explanation: This intermediate example demonstrates: 1) Entity classes representing core domain objects (Product, Category), 2) User hierarchy with inheritance (User -> Customer), 3) Composition relationships (Cart contains CartItems, Order contains items), 4) Service layer for business logic (OrderService), 5) Encapsulation of data and behaviors
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for cart operations, O(n) for order search
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing orders and cart items

examples.title: Advanced: Design Patterns from Requirements Analysis
examples.description: Shows how to apply design patterns (Factory, Observer, Strategy) based on specific requirements
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates three design patterns derived from requirements: 1) Strategy Pattern - Different discount calculations based on customer type, 2) Factory Pattern - Creating payment objects without exposing instantiation logic, 3) Observer Pattern - Notifying multiple channels (email, SMS) after payment. This shows how requirements analysis leads to appropriate design pattern selection
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for payment processing, O(n) for observer notification
